data.title: Investors Still Aren't Entirely Convinced By Movida Participações S.A.'s (BVMF:MOVI3) Revenues Despite 33% Price Jump
data.description:
Movida Participações S.A. (BVMF:MOVI3) shares have continued their recent momentum with a 33% gain in the last month alone. The last month tops off a massive increase of 230% in the last year.

Even after such a large jump in price, when close to half the companies operating in Brazil's Transportation industry have price-to-sales ratios (or "P/S") above 1x, you may still consider Movida Participações as an enticing stock to check out with its 0.3x P/S ratio. Nonetheless, we'd need to dig a little deeper to determine if there is a rational basis for the reduced P/S.

What Does Movida Participações' P/S Mean For Shareholders?

Movida Participações could be doing better as it's been growing revenue less than most other companies lately. The P/S ratio is probably low because investors think this lacklustre revenue performance isn't going to get any better. If this is the case, then existing shareholders will probably struggle to get excited about the future direction of the share price.

Keen to find out how analysts think Movida Participações' future stacks up against the industry? In that case, our free report is a great place to start.

Do Revenue Forecasts Match The Low P/S Ratio?

The only time you'd be truly comfortable seeing a P/S as low as Movida Participações' is when the company's growth is on track to lag the industry.

Taking a look back first, we see that the company managed to grow revenues by a handy 12% last year. The latest three year period has also seen an excellent 69% overall rise in revenue, aided somewhat by its short-term performance. So we can start by confirming that the company has done a great job of growing revenues over that time.

Shifting to the future, estimates from the twelve analysts covering the company suggest revenue should grow by 11% per year over the next three years. Meanwhile, the rest of the industry is forecast to only expand by 8.1% each year, which is noticeably less attractive.

In light of this, it's peculiar that Movida Participações' P/S sits below the majority of other companies. Apparently some shareholders are doubtful of the forecasts and have been accepting significantly lower selling prices.

What Does Movida Participações' P/S Mean For Investors?

Movida Participações' stock price has surged recently, but its but its P/S still remains modest. Generally, our preference is to limit the use of the price-to-sales ratio to establishing what the market thinks about the overall health of a company.

Movida Participações' analyst forecasts revealed that its superior revenue outlook isn't contributing to its P/S anywhere near as much as we would have predicted. There could be some major risk factors that are placing downward pressure on the P/S ratio. At least price risks look to be very low, but investors seem to think future revenues could see a lot of volatility.

data.title: Return Trends At Equatorial Maranhão Distribuidora de Energia (BVMF:EQMA3B) Aren't Appealing
data.description:
Finding a business that has the potential to grow substantially is not easy, but it is possible if we look at a few key financial metrics. In a perfect world, we'd like to see a company investing more capital into its business and ideally the returns earned from that capital are also increasing. If you see this, it typically means it's a company with a great business model and plenty of profitable reinvestment opportunities. So, when we ran our eye over Equatorial Maranhão Distribuidora de Energia's (BVMF:EQMA3B) trend of ROCE, we liked what we saw.

We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

Advertisement

What Is Return On Capital Employed (ROCE)?

Just to clarify if you're unsure, ROCE is a metric for evaluating how much pre-tax income (in percentage terms) a company earns on the capital invested in its business. Analysts use this formula to calculate it for Equatorial Maranhão Distribuidora de Energia:

Return on Capital Employed = Earnings Before Interest and Tax (EBIT) ÷ (Total Assets - Current Liabilities)

0.13 = R$1.2b ÷ (R$12b - R$2.3b) (Based on the trailing twelve months to September 2025).

Therefore, Equatorial Maranhão Distribuidora de Energia has an ROCE of 13%. That's a relatively normal return on capital, and it's around the 12% generated by the Electric Utilities industry.

Check out our latest analysis for Equatorial Maranhão Distribuidora de Energia

Historical performance is a great place to start when researching a stock so above you can see the gauge for Equatorial Maranhão Distribuidora de Energia's ROCE against it's prior returns. If you're interested in investigating Equatorial Maranhão Distribuidora de Energia's past further, check out this free graph covering Equatorial Maranhão Distribuidora de Energia's past earnings, revenue and cash flow.

What The Trend Of ROCE Can Tell Us

While the returns on capital are good, they haven't moved much. The company has employed 59% more capital in the last five years, and the returns on that capital have remained stable at 13%. 13% is a pretty standard return, and it provides some comfort knowing that Equatorial Maranhão Distribuidora de Energia has consistently earned this amount. Stable returns in this ballpark can be unexciting, but if they can be maintained over the long run, they often provide nice rewards to shareholders.

The Key Takeaway

The main thing to remember is that Equatorial Maranhão Distribuidora de Energia has proven its ability to continually reinvest at respectable rates of return. However, over the last five years, the stock has only delivered a 24% return to shareholders who held over that period. That's why it could be worth your time looking into this stock further to discover if it has more traits of a multi-bagger.

data.title: We Think Empreendimentos Pague Menos (BVMF:PGMN3) Is Taking Some Risk With Its Debt
data.description:
David Iben put it well when he said, 'Volatility is not a risk we care about. What we care about is avoiding the permanent loss of capital.' It's only natural to consider a company's balance sheet when you examine how risky it is, since debt is often involved when a business collapses. We can see that Empreendimentos Pague Menos S.A. (BVMF:PGMN3) does use debt in its business. But the more important question is: how much risk is that debt creating?

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

Advertisement

When Is Debt Dangerous?

Debt assists a business until the business has trouble paying it off, either with new capital or with free cash flow. Part and parcel of capitalism is the process of 'creative destruction' where failed businesses are mercilessly liquidated by their bankers. However, a more frequent (but still costly) occurrence is where a company must issue shares at bargain-basement prices, permanently diluting shareholders, just to shore up its balance sheet. Of course, debt can be an important tool in businesses, particularly capital heavy businesses. When we examine debt levels, we first consider both cash and debt levels, together.

How Much Debt Does Empreendimentos Pague Menos Carry?

As you can see below, at the end of September 2025, Empreendimentos Pague Menos had R$1.75b of debt, up from R$1.46b a year ago. Click the image for more detail. However, it also had R$106.3m in cash, and so its net debt is R$1.64b.

How Strong Is Empreendimentos Pague Menos' Balance Sheet?

The latest balance sheet data shows that Empreendimentos Pague Menos had liabilities of R$3.28b due within a year, and liabilities of R$3.13b falling due after that. Offsetting these obligations, it had cash of R$106.3m as well as receivables valued at R$1.45b due within 12 months. So its liabilities outweigh the sum of its cash and (near-term) receivables by R$4.85b.

Given this deficit is actually higher than the company's market capitalization of R$4.25b, we think shareholders really should watch Empreendimentos Pague Menos's debt levels, like a parent watching their child ride a bike for the first time. Hypothetically, extremely heavy dilution would be required if the company were forced to pay down its liabilities by raising capital at the current share price.

See our latest analysis for Empreendimentos Pague Menos

We measure a company's debt load relative to its earnings power by looking at its net debt divided by its earnings before interest, tax, depreciation, and amortization (EBITDA) and by calculating how easily its earnings before interest and tax (EBIT) cover its interest expense (interest cover). This way, we consider both the absolute quantum of the debt, as well as the interest rates paid on it.

While Empreendimentos Pague Menos has a quite reasonable net debt to EBITDA multiple of 2.4, its interest cover seems weak, at 1.7. This does suggest the company is paying fairly high interest rates. In any case, it's safe to say the company has meaningful debt. Importantly, Empreendimentos Pague Menos grew its EBIT by 37% over the last twelve months, and that growth will make it easier to handle its debt. When analysing debt levels, the balance sheet is the obvious place to start. But it is future earnings, more than anything, that will determine Empreendimentos Pague Menos's ability to maintain a healthy balance sheet going forward. So if you're focused on the future you can check out this free report showing analyst profit forecasts.

Finally, while the tax-man may adore accounting profits, lenders only accept cold hard cash. So we clearly need to look at whether that EBIT is leading to corresponding free cash flow. Over the last three years, Empreendimentos Pague Menos reported free cash flow worth 19% of its EBIT, which is really quite low. For us, cash conversion that low sparks a little paranoia about is ability to extinguish debt.

Our View

On the face of it, Empreendimentos Pague Menos's level of total liabilities left us tentative about the stock, and its interest cover was no more enticing than the one empty restaurant on the busiest night of the year. But on the bright side, its EBIT growth rate is a good sign, and makes us more optimistic. Once we consider all the factors above, together, it seems to us that Empreendimentos Pague Menos's debt is making it a bit risky. That's not necessarily a bad thing, but we'd generally feel more comfortable with less leverage. The balance sheet is clearly the area to focus on when you are analysing debt. But ultimately, every company can contain risks that exist outside of the balance sheet. We've identified 4 warning signs with Empreendimentos Pague Menos (at least 2 which are a bit concerning) , and understanding them should be part of your investment process.

At the end of the day, it's often better to focus on companies that are free from net debt. You can access our special list of such companies (all with a track record of profit growth). It's free.

data.title: Park National (PRK) Margin Expansion Reinforces Bullish Narratives Despite P/E Premium
data.description:
Park National (PRK) has rounded out FY 2025 with fourth quarter revenue of US$140.5 million and basic EPS of US$2.65, alongside trailing twelve month revenue of US$545.7 million and EPS of US$11.18 that sit against an 18.9% earnings increase over the past year. Over the last few quarters, revenue has moved from US$130.6 million in Q4 2024 to US$137.6 million in Q3 2025 and US$140.5 million in Q4 2025, while quarterly EPS has shifted from US$2.39 to US$2.93 and then US$2.65. This sets up a picture where higher trailing net margins and that earnings growth profile are central to how investors read the new results.

See our full analysis for Park National.

With the fresh numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings run rate and margin profile line up with the prevailing narratives around Park National, and where the story in the data pushes back on those views.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

33% net margin puts profits in focus

  • Over the last twelve months, Park National generated US$180.1 million of net income on US$545.7 million of revenue, which works out to a 33% net profit margin compared with 29.9% a year earlier.
  • What stands out for the bullish view is that this 18.9% earnings growth over the past year sits alongside a 33% margin and a net interest margin that was between 4.62% and 4.75% in the first three quarters of 2025. This heavily supports the idea of a solid underlying banking franchise but also raises questions about how repeatable this step up is given five year average earnings growth of about 2% a year.
    • Supporters can point to quarterly net income between US$42.2 million and US$48.1 million across 2025 and a cost to income ratio that stayed in a mid 50% to low 60% range as evidence that the bank is keeping a decent handle on costs while converting revenue to profit.
    • On the other hand, critics of the bullish angle may argue that with earnings growth forecasts of about 8.8% a year, the recent 18.9% gain looks like a strong year rather than a long term trend, so they may want to see how margins behave across future reporting periods.

P/E premium and DCF gap pull in different directions

  • Park National is trading on a trailing P/E of 14.4x versus 11.8x for the wider US Banks industry and 12.5x for peers, while the DCF fair value in the data is US$244.02 against the current share price of US$161.10. This suggests the stock is about 34% below that DCF fair value estimate and also sits below an analyst price target of US$180.33.
  • Here, the bullish narrative leans on the combination of that 34% gap to the DCF fair value and the 2.66% dividend yield. Yet the same data also shows the stock carrying a P/E premium, which challenges anyone arguing that the shares look cheap on all measures.
    • Supporters of the bullish case might highlight that trailing EPS of US$11.18, if it holds, means the current P/E of 14.4x is being applied to a business that just posted an 18.9% earnings increase with a 33% net margin, which they may see as consistent with paying somewhat more than the sector average P/E multiple.
    • Skeptics, however, can point out that even though the model based DCF fair value comes in at US$244.02, the forecasts in the data show earnings and revenue growth that are slower than the broader US market, which they may view as a reason to question how much weight to put on that fair value figure compared with the peer and industry P/E levels.
Curious how the recent margin improvement and valuation trade offs stack up against longer term market views on Park National? 📊 Read the full Park National Consensus Narrative.

Loan book scale and credit quality under the microscope

  • Total loans sat around US$7.9b across 2024 and 2025, with reported balances between US$7,731.0 million and US$7,993.0 million, while non performing loans in the quarterly data ranged from US$63.1 million to US$90.6 million, giving investors a sense of both the size of the book and the level of stressed exposures being carried.
  • For a cautious, more bearish leaning view, the key tension is that while the bank is earning a net interest margin as high as 4.75% in 2025 and keeping its cost to income ratio between about 55% and 63%, the presence of up to US$90.6 million of non performing loans and a loan book close to US$7.9b means credit quality remains an area to track closely even as profitability metrics look healthy.
    • Those who worry about the bearish side of the story may argue that a higher P/E of 14.4x alongside non performing loans above US$60 million gives them less room for comfort if credit costs rise, and they may look at how that interacts with earnings forecasts that are below the broader US market growth

data.title: IonQ’s SkyWater Deal Aims To Build Secure Full Stack Quantum Platform
data.description:
• IonQ (NYSE:IONQ) has agreed to acquire SkyWater Technology in a cash and stock deal valued at about US$1.8b.
  • The transaction will create what IonQ describes as a vertically integrated, full stack quantum computing and semiconductor platform.
  • SkyWater will become a wholly owned subsidiary, keeping its foundry services while adding quantum focused offerings.

For you as an investor, this deal connects IonQ’s quantum computing focus directly with a U.S. based semiconductor foundry, potentially reducing reliance on external chip suppliers. The combined company operates across quantum hardware, networking, security, and sensing, with relevance for government, defense, and aerospace use cases.

IonQ’s move also links quantum development more closely to a domestic manufacturing base, which is an important theme for policymakers and large enterprise buyers. As the integration progresses, investors may track how the company executes on this full stack setup and how it affects development of large scale, fault tolerant quantum processors.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for IonQ by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on IonQ.

Why IonQ could be great value

For IonQ, folding SkyWater into the group directly ties its quantum roadmap to a trusted, U.S. based fabrication base, which could matter a lot for customers in aerospace, defense, and other sensitive sectors. The company is effectively betting that tighter control over chip design, manufacturing, and packaging will help it iterate quantum processors faster and position its federal division as a one stop shop for high security quantum hardware and related semiconductors.

Advertisement

How This Deal Plays Into The IonQ Narrative

IonQ has already been building a story around technical milestones like 99.99% two qubit fidelity, aggressive acquisition activity, and deeper government ties, including the appointment of national security focused executives such as Katie Arrington and an expanded CISO role. data.title: FNS TECH (KOSDAQ:083500) Has A Rock Solid Balance Sheet
data.description:
Warren Buffett famously said, 'Volatility is far from synonymous with risk.' It's only natural to consider a company's balance sheet when you examine how risky it is, since debt is often involved when a business collapses. We note that FNS TECH. Co., Ltd (KOSDAQ:083500) does have debt on its balance sheet. But the more important question is: how much risk is that debt creating?

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

Advertisement

When Is Debt A Problem?

Debt and other liabilities become risky for a business when it cannot easily fulfill those obligations, either with free cash flow or by raising capital at an attractive price. Ultimately, if the company can't fulfill its legal obligations to repay debt, shareholders could walk away with nothing. However, a more common (but still painful) scenario is that it has to raise new equity capital at a low price, thus permanently diluting shareholders. Of course, debt can be an important tool in businesses, particularly capital heavy businesses. When we think about a company's use of debt, we first look at cash and debt together.

What Is FNS TECH's Debt?

You can click the graphic below for the historical numbers, but it shows that FNS TECH had ₩19.4b of debt in September 2025, down from ₩20.4b, one year before. But it also has ₩27.4b in cash to offset that, meaning it has ₩7.98b net cash.

A Look At FNS TECH's Liabilities

We can see from the most recent balance sheet that FNS TECH had liabilities of ₩24.5b falling due within a year, and liabilities of ₩8.21b due beyond that. On the other hand, it had cash of ₩27.4b and ₩7.92b worth of receivables due within a year. So it actually has ₩2.58b more liquid assets than total liabilities.

This short term liquidity is a sign that FNS TECH could probably pay off its debt with ease, as its balance sheet is far from stretched. Simply put, the fact that FNS TECH has more cash than debt is arguably a good indication that it can manage its debt safely.

See our latest analysis for FNS TECH

Better yet, FNS TECH grew its EBIT by 128% last year, which is an impressive improvement. That boost will make it even easier to pay down debt going forward. There's no doubt that we learn most about debt from the balance sheet. But ultimately the future profitability of the business will decide if FNS TECH can strengthen its balance sheet over time. So if you want to see what the professionals think, you might find this free report on analyst profit forecasts to be interesting.

Finally, a company can only pay off debt with cold hard cash, not accounting profits. While FNS TECH has net cash on its balance sheet, it's still worth taking a look at its ability to convert earnings before interest and tax (EBIT) to free cash flow, to help us understand how quickly it is building (or eroding) that cash balance. During the last three years, FNS TECH produced sturdy free cash flow equating to 54% of its EBIT, about what we'd expect. This free cash flow puts the company in a good position to pay down debt, when appropriate.

Summing Up

While it is always sensible to investigate a company's debt, in this case FNS TECH has ₩7.98b in net cash and a decent-looking balance sheet. And it impressed us with its EBIT growth of 128% over the last year. So we don't think FNS TECH's use of debt is risky. The balance sheet is clearly the area to focus on when you are analysing debt. But ultimately, every company can contain risks that exist outside of the balance sheet. We've identified 2 warning signs with FNS TECH , and understanding them should be part of your investment process.

data.title: Little Excitement Around Keller Group plc's (LON:KLR) Earnings
data.description:
With a price-to-earnings (or "P/E") ratio of 8.7x Keller Group plc (LON:KLR) may be sending bullish signals at the moment, given that almost half of all companies in the United Kingdom have P/E ratios greater than 17x and even P/E's higher than 29x are not unusual. Nonetheless, we'd need to dig a little deeper to determine if there is a rational basis for the reduced P/E.

Trump has pledged to "unleash" American oil and gas and these 15 US stocks have developments that are poised to benefit.

With earnings growth that's superior to most other companies of late, Keller Group has been doing relatively well. One possibility is that the P/E is low because investors think this strong earnings performance might be less impressive moving forward. If not, then existing shareholders have reason to be quite optimistic about the future direction of the share price.

See our latest analysis for Keller Group

If you'd like to see what analysts are forecasting going forward, you should check out our free report on Keller Group.
Advertisement

Is There Any Growth For Keller Group?

In order to justify its P/E ratio, Keller Group would need to produce sluggish growth that's trailing the market.

If we review the last year of earnings growth, the company posted a worthy increase of 12%. This was backed up an excellent period prior to see EPS up by 170% in total over the last three years. Accordingly, shareholders would have probably welcomed those medium-term rates of earnings growth.

Shifting to the future, estimates from the six analysts covering the company suggest earnings should grow by 4.2% per annum over the next three years. Meanwhile, the rest of the market is forecast to expand by 15% per year, which is noticeably more attractive.

With this information, we can see why Keller Group is trading at a P/E lower than the market. It seems most investors are expecting to see limited future growth and are only willing to pay a reduced amount for the stock.

The Bottom Line On Keller Group's P/E

Using the price-to-earnings ratio alone to determine if you should sell your stock isn't sensible, however it can be a practical guide to the company's future prospects.

As we suspected, our examination of Keller Group's analyst forecasts revealed that its inferior earnings outlook is contributing to its low P/E. Right now shareholders are accepting the low P/E as they concede future earnings probably won't provide any pleasant surprises. data.title: While shareholders of Future (LON:FUTR) are in the red over the last five years, underlying earnings have actually grown
data.description:
It's nice to see the Future plc (LON:FUTR) share price up 11% in a week. But that is little comfort to those holding over the last half decade, sitting on a big loss. In fact, the share price has declined rather badly, down some 70% in that time. Some might say the recent bounce is to be expected after such a bad drop. But it could be that the fall was overdone.

The recent uptick of 11% could be a positive sign of things to come, so let's take a look at historical fundamentals.

We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

In his essay The Superinvestors of Graham-and-Doddsville Warren Buffett described how share prices do not always rationally reflect the value of a business. One imperfect but simple way to consider how the market perception of a company has shifted is to compare the change in the earnings per share (EPS) with the share price movement.

While the share price declined over five years, Future actually managed to increase EPS by an average of 8.9% per year. Given the share price reaction, one might suspect that EPS is not a good guide to the business performance during the period (perhaps due to a one-off loss or gain). Or possibly, the market was previously very optimistic, so the stock has disappointed, despite improving EPS.

Because of the sharp contrast between the EPS growth rate and the share price growth, we're inclined to look to other metrics to understand the changing market sentiment around the stock.

In contrast to the share price, revenue has actually increased by 9.6% a year in the five year period. A more detailed examination of the revenue and earnings may or may not explain why the share price languishes; there could be an opportunity.

You can see below how earnings and revenue have changed over time (discover the exact values by clicking on the image).

It's good to see that there was some significant insider buying in the last three months. That's a positive. On the other hand, we think the revenue and earnings trends are much more meaningful measures of the business. So we recommend checking out this free report showing consensus forecasts

Advertisement

A Different Perspective

Investors in Future had a tough year, with a total loss of 41% (including dividends), against a market gain of about 26%. Even the share prices of good stocks drop sometimes, but we want to see improvements in the fundamental metrics of a business, before getting too interested. Regrettably, last year's performance caps off a bad run, with the shareholders facing a total loss of 11% per year over five years. Generally speaking long term share price weakness can be a bad sign, though contrarian investors might want to research the stock in hope of a turnaround. I find it very interesting to look at share price over the long term as a proxy for business performance. But to truly gain insight, we need to consider other information, too. Consider for instance, the ever-present spectre of investment risk. We've identified 1 warning sign with Future , and understanding them should be part of your investment process.

data.title: With EPS Growth And More, REN - Redes Energéticas Nacionais SGPS (ELI:RENE) Makes An Interesting Case
data.description:
Investors are often guided by the idea of discovering 'the next big thing', even if that means buying 'story stocks' without any revenue, let alone profit. But the reality is that when a company loses money each year, for long enough, its investors will usually take their share of those losses. A loss-making company is yet to prove itself with profit, and eventually the inflow of external capital may dry up.

So if this idea of high risk and high reward doesn't suit, you might be more interested in profitable, growing companies, like REN - Redes Energéticas Nacionais SGPS (ELI:RENE). Now this is not to say that the company presents the best investment opportunity around, but profitability is a key component to success in business.

Advertisement

How Fast Is REN - Redes Energéticas Nacionais SGPS Growing?

The market is a voting machine in the short term, but a weighing machine in the long term, so you'd expect share price to follow earnings per share (EPS) outcomes eventually. So it makes sense that experienced investors pay close attention to company EPS when undertaking investment research. We can see that in the last three years REN - Redes Energéticas Nacionais SGPS grew its EPS by 16% per year. That's a pretty good rate, if the company can sustain it.

Careful consideration of revenue growth and earnings before interest and taxation (EBIT) margins can help inform a view on the sustainability of the recent profit growth. On the revenue front, REN - Redes Energéticas Nacionais SGPS has done well over the past year, growing revenue by 8.2% to €1.0b but EBIT margin figures were less stellar, seeing a decline over the last 12 months. If EBIT margins are able to stay balanced and this revenue growth continues, then we should see brighter days ahead.

Are REN - Redes Energéticas Nacionais SGPS Insiders Aligned With All Shareholders?

Prior to investment, it's always a good idea to check that the management team is paid reasonably. Pay levels around or below the median, can be a sign that shareholder interests are well considered. The median total compensation for CEOs of companies similar in size to REN - Redes Energéticas Nacionais SGPS, with market caps between €1.7b and €5.3b, is around €1.2m.

The REN - Redes Energéticas Nacionais SGPS CEO received €848k in compensation for the year ending December 2024. That seems pretty reasonable, especially given it's below the median for similar sized companies. While the level of CEO compensation shouldn't be the biggest factor in how the company is viewed, modest remuneration is a positive, because it suggests that the board keeps shareholder interests in mind. Generally, arguments can be made that reasonable pay levels attest to good decision-making.

Should You Add REN - Redes Energéticas Nacionais SGPS To Your Watchlist?

As previously touched on, REN - Redes Energéticas Nacionais SGPS is a growing business, which is encouraging.
